Off Calendar May-11-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances (via ZOOM videoconference):
Atty. Jaeman Chang, Esq. of Fox Rothschild LLP, 415.364.5540, for Plaintiff Jeannie Tsai;
Atty. Nevin C. Brownfield, Esq. of Brownfield Law P.C., 415.987.7501, for Defendant Nevin C. Brownfield;
Atty. Steven McLellan, Esq. of Gates Eisenhart Dawson, 408.288.8100, for Defendant Dawn Sherpa.

DEFENDANT DAWN SERPA'S MOTION TO STRIKE AMENDED COMPLAINT.

The matter is called. ARGUMENT is held. At the conclusion of argument, the tentative ruling is adopted as modified:

Serpa's motion to strike is denied as to the purported "puffery" or "opinion" allegations. The allegations are not false, improper, or irrelevant per CCP sec. 436(a). A factual issue remains whether Serpa possessed "superior knowledge" such that these allegations can support plaintiff's fraud claims. (See Brakke v. Economic Concepts, Inc. (2013) 213 Cal.App.4th 761, 769 [explaining when opinion can support fraud claim]; Jolley v. Chase Home Finance, LLC (2013) 213 Cal.App.4th 872, 892 [same].) The motion to strike is granted without leave to amend as to paragraphs 135, 136, and 137. These paragraphs allege violations of various securities laws to support the ninth cause of action. All of the claims are time barred, however, and they are therefore legal conclusions and irrelevant. The simple allegations at paragraphs 135-137 that Serpa violated securities laws do not support the fraud claims. Indeed, those allegations are not incorporated into the fraud claims.

Order signed as modified.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda; Court Reporter: Rose Miller, CSR No. 5041, Email:RMillerCSR@gmail.com. (302/RBU)

DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar May-11-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances (via ZOOM videoconference):
Atty. Jaeman Chang, Esq. of Fox Rothschild LLP, 415.364.5540, for Plaintiff Jeannie Tsai;
Atty. Nevin C. Brownfield, Esq. of Brownfield Law P.C., 415.987.7501, for Defendant Nevin C. Brownfield;
Atty. Steven McLellan, Esq. of Gates Eisenhart Dawson, 408.288.8100, for Defendant Dawn Sherpa.

DEFENDANT DAWN SERPA'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT.

The matter is called. NO ARGUMENT is held on this Demurrer. The tentative ruling is adopted as follows:

Serpa's demurrer to amended complaint is sustained and overruled as follows:

The demurrer to cause of action 3 for "Fraud in the Execution or Inception" is sustained without leave to amend. (CCP sec. 430.10(e).) The allegations of the amended complaint show that plaintiff knew she was entering into a loan with the defendants and was fraudulently induced to do so based on defendants' alleged misrepresentations. (See, e.g., Amended Complaint, pars. 3-4.) Plaintiff pleads a classic fraud in the inducement claim and there are no facts to support a fraud in the execution claim. In Rosenthal v. Great Western Fin. Securities Corp. (1996) 14 Cal.4th 394, 415, the court explains that fraud in the execution exists where the party "does not know what he is signing" while "fraud in the inducement occurs when the promisor knows what he is signing but his consent is induced by fraud." (emphasis in original.)

The demurrer to cause of action 4 for "Fraud in the Factum" is sustained without leave to amend. (CCP sec. 430.10(e).) The title of this claim is also a fraud in the execution claim, but plaintiff has clarified that it is really a CC sec. 1572 "catch all" fraud claim. (See Fruestta v. Hauben (1990) 217 Cal.App.3d 551, 556 ["Professor Williston defines fraud in the factum as 'such fraud in the procurement of the execution of a note or bill as results in the signer's being ignorant of the nature of the instrument he is signing'."].) Notwithstanding plaintiff's clarification, the Court dismisses the claim as duplicative. There is no salient difference between causes of action 4 and 2 and plaintiff can vindicate her rights by prosecuting cause of action 2.

The demurrer to cause of action 9 for "Securities Violations" is sustained without leave to amend. (See CCP sec. 430.10(e).) Plaintiff concedes that the claim/s are time barred.

The demurrer to cause of action 10 for "Breach of Promissory Note" is overruled. Per CCP sec. 452, the Court liberally reads the amended complaint and plaintiff pleads the existence of a written contract. A plain reading of the complaint indicates that the parties entered into a written agreement memorialized by a "note." A "promissory note" has long been held to be a "contract in writing and in the event of an ambiguity appearing on its face is to be construed as any other written contract." (Trigg v. Arnott (1937) 22 Cal.App.2d 455, 457; see also Saks v. Charity Mission Baptist Church (2001) 90 Cal.App.4th 1116, 1132 [promissory note is a signed negotiable instrument].) Serpa's reply argues that the cause of action does not sufficiently allege the terms of the contract or attach a copy. That is a new argument based on CCP 430.10(e) and the demurrer was based on CCP 430.10(g). The new argument is rejected based on due process grounds.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda; Court Reporter: Rose Miller, CSR No. 5041, Email: RMillerCSR@gmail.com. (302/RBU)
